topic.what: Systematic review of the evidence for proton pump inhibitor (PPI)-induced nutrient deficiencies, specifically covering magnesium, calcium, iron, vitamin B12, and vitamin C, and the mechanisms by which gastric acid suppression causes these deficiencies.
topic.how: Narrative synthesis of published observational studies, case series, and mechanistic research; evaluates each micronutrient separately, examining the role of gastric acid in absorption, evidence of depletion with PPI use, and clinical consequences. Supports the hypochlorhydria model as a unifying mechanism.
topic.why_matters: Long-term PPI use is among the most common medication exposures worldwide; nutrient depletion has downstream consequences for bone density, cardiac function, neurological health, and immune competence. Provides actionable evidence for monitoring and supplementation in PPI users and reinforces the broader argument that acid suppression has systemic costs.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Number of distinct micronutrients reviewed for PPI-associated depletion
characteristics.vs_baseline: Earlier reviews typically focused on single nutrients (e.g., calcium/fractures alone)
characteristics.evidence: Structured narrative review with primary literature citations
characteristics.context: Survey design; effect sizes drawn from heterogeneous underlying studies

characteristics.what_it_measures: Severity and prevalence of PPI-associated hypomagnesaemia
characteristics.value: Clinically significant — FDA warning issued; cardiac arrhythmia risk documented
characteristics.vs_baseline: Magnesium depletion not on prescribers' radar before FDA 2011 safety communication
characteristics.evidence: Case reports and pharmacovigilance data; FDA Drug Safety Communication 2011
characteristics.context: Effect most pronounced with long-term high-dose PPI use

characteristics.what_it_measures: Degree to which gastric acid role in nutrient absorption is mechanistically explained
characteristics.value: Moderate — acid-dependent solubilization explained for each nutrient
characteristics.vs_baseline: Most clinical PPI literature focuses on efficacy, not absorption physiology
characteristics.evidence: Biochemical rationale plus supporting human studies
characteristics.context: Causal inference limited by observational study designs in primary sources

good_for: Clinicians monitoring long-term PPI users for micronutrient status
Building the evidence case for acid suppression as a cause of systemic nutrient deficiency
Informing supplementation protocols in patients on chronic PPI therapy
Supporting the hypochlorhydria-as-harm framing in functional medicine contexts
not_for: Patients where PPI benefits clearly outweigh risks (e.g., Barrett's esophagus, active bleeding ulcer)
Quantitative pooled effect sizes — narrative review only
requires: Baseline micronutrient testing in patients considered for long-term PPI initiation
Awareness that effect sizes vary by PPI agent, dose, and duration

novelty: Consolidated evidence for multiple PPI-associated nutrient deficiencies in a single review, enabling cross-nutrient comparison
Highlighted the clinical significance of PPI-induced hypomagnesaemia at a time when it was underrecognized
Mechanistically linked gastric acid suppression to impaired nutrient solubilization and absorption across five distinct pathways
open_problems: Optimal monitoring frequency and thresholds for micronutrient screening in long-term PPI users are not standardized
Whether supplementation fully corrects PPI-induced deficiencies or acid restoration is required remains unclear
Individual variation in susceptibility to PPI-induced nutrient depletion is poorly characterized
Interaction effects between simultaneous deficiencies (e.g., B12 + magnesium on neurological risk) are unstudied
